coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from elm seeds via hydrothermal method for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jianbin
Zheng, Guoshuang
Tian, Yan
Zhang, Chenghong
Wang, Yuting
Liu, Mingjie
Ren, Dongze
Sun, Huijun
Yu, Weiting
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal method to prepare biomass-based carbon dots (CDs) is environmentally-friendly and it can convert useless waste into valuable materials. In this work, we aimed to use natural siberian elm (Ulmus pumila L.) seeds as carbon source to synthesize CDs by hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR and XPS methods, founding they were near-spherical in shape with an average diameter of 3.83 nm. The CDs were mainly composed by carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, which formed the various hydrophilic functional carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and amide groups on the surface. In addition, the CDs could emit bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination with excellent pH stability and photostability. Among various metal ions, the CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+. The degree of quenching was linearly related to the concentration of Fe3+, indicating they could be used for Fe3+ detection. Finally, the CDs were proven to be good biocompatible by MTT assay and they could effectively label HepG2 cells with bright blue fluorescence. In summary, the CDs could be potentially applied as Fe3+ sensors for water pollution detection and agents for cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Elm seeds
Fe3+ detection
Cell imaging
Hydrothermal method
Biomass

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) HRTEM image of CDs, (C) the size distribution of CDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of CDs, and high resolution XPS (B) C1s, (C) O1s and (D) N1s spectra of CDs.

(A) Appearance of CDs solution under daylight and UV light. (B) UV–vis absorption (blue line), fluorescence excitation (Ex, green line) and emission (Em, orange line) spectra of CDs. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different excitation wavelength.
(A) pH stability and (B) photostability of CDs.

(A) Selectivity of CDs on Fe3+ over other metal ions, F0 and F present the fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after adding metal ions. (B) Digital photographs of CDs solution with or without Fe3+ under UV light. (C) The effect of Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. (D) The linear calibration curve of F/F0 value versus Fe3+ concentration.
(A) Cellular viabilities of HepG2 cells in the present of different concentrations of CDs.
Fluorescence images of HepG2 cells incubated with CDs for 12 h, under bright field and 405 nm laser irradiation.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of CDs from elm seeds and the applications for Fe3+ detection and bioimaging.
Comparison of the capability of Fe3+ detection by in the present CDs with various reported biomass-based CDs.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from elm seeds via hydrothermal method for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Affiliated Zhongshan Hospital of Dalian University, 6 Jiefang Street, Dalian 116001, China (W. Yu). College of Pharmacy, Dalian Medical University, 9 West Sect Lvshun South Rd, Dalian 116044, China (H. Sun).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Zhongshan Hospital of Dalian University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jianbin Zhang and Guoshuang Zheng contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal method to prepare biomass-based carbon dots (CDs) is environmentally-friendly and it can convert useless waste into valuable materials. In this work, we aimed to use natural siberian elm (Ulmus pumila L.) seeds as carbon source to synthesize CDs by hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR and XPS methods, founding they were near-spherical in shape with an average diameter of 3.83 nm. The CDs were mainly composed by carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, which formed the various hydrophilic functional carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and amide groups on the surface. In addition, the CDs could emit bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet illumination with excellent pH stability and photostability. Among various metal ions, the CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+. The degree of quenching was linearly related to the concentration of Fe3+, indicating they could be used for Fe3+ detection. Finally, the CDs were proven to be good biocompatible by MTT assay and they could effectively label HepG2 cells with bright blue fluorescence. In summary, the CDs could be potentially applied as Fe3+ sensors for water pollution detection and agents for cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are fascinating zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon materials with particle sizes less than 10 nm [1]. They have been widely studies for near 20 years since firstly reported in 2004 [2], because they possess numerous unique properties such as outstanding hydrophilicity, good photostability, adjustable fluorescence, easy modifiability, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, photocatalytic activity and antibacterial activity [3]. Therefore, CDs are extensively applied in the fields of sensors, photocatalysis, supercapacitors, bioimaging, drug delivery and antibacterial agents [4,5]. Up to now, CDs are mainly prepared by two strategies: “top-down” or “bottom-up”. “Top-down” strategies usually obtain CDs by decomposing bulky carbon materials by physical or chemical treatments, including arc discharge, laser ablation or electrochemical processing methods. However, these methods always require expensive equipments and complex conditions. “Bottom-up” strategies can synthesis CDs from carbon-rich compounds with a series of chemical reactions, such as template, microwave, pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods [6]. Among these methods, hydrothermal method is the most commonly used because of the advantages of low cost, environmentally-friendly and mass-production [7]. Various CDs have been reported synthesized by hydrothermal method, with the carbon sources can be selected as chemical materials (i.e. citric acid, ethanediamine and phenylenediamine) or biomass (i.e. food, flowers, leaves, fruits and biological waste) [8,9]. Furthermore, the fluorescence properties of CDs can be improved by doping certain heteroatom (such as N, S, F, P and B).

In recent years, biomass-based CDs have attracted great attention, as biomass is abundant, renewable, biocompatible and inexpensive [10,11]. The synthesis of CDs from biomass via hydrothermal method is considered as environmentally-friendly and it can convert useless waste into valuable materials [12]. In addition, biomass contained abundant heteroatom like N, S, P, which is beneficial to improve the property of CDs. Many types of biomass have been reported to produce CDs, such as milk, juice, Manihot esculenta, Prunus cerasifera fruits, Lonicera maackii fruit, Calotropis gigantean leaves and corn stalk shell [7,12–15]. The obtained CDs were widely used for multi-applications: mental ions sensor, bioimaging, fluorescent ink, drug delivery, and so on [16–18].

Trivalent iron ion (Fe3+) is the most abundant trace metal ion in human body and plays an important role in multiple physiological processes, such as serving as enzyme cofactor in catalysis, involving in cellular metabolism, and carrying oxygen in hemoglobin [13]. Abnormal Fe3+ level can induce various physiological disorders: excess Fe3+ can cause anaemia, heart failure, diabetics, liver and kidney, while Fe3+ deficiency leads to anemia diseases, decreased immunity and human neurological disorders [19]. Hence, detection of Fe3+ concentration in the drinking water, food, waste and environment pollution is essential for human health. Numerous biomass-based CDs have been reported as Fe3+ sensors with very high sensitivity and selectivity.

Siberian elm (Ulmus pumila L.) is a tree widely cultivated throughout Asia, North America, Argentina, and southern Europe [20]. The unripe elm seeds are rich in nutrients, and could be eaten by people as food or medicine [21–23]. Once the elm seeds are mature, they are dispersed with wind and become biomass waste. In order to avoid the waste of elm seeds, CDs might be one of promising route to raise their utilizing value. However, as far as we know, the synthesis of CDs from elm seeds has not been reported.

In this work, for the first time, we developed novel CDs using elm seeds as carbon source via hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) radiation with excellent aqueous solubility. Their characterizations were further measured by TEM, FTIR, XPS, UV and fluorescence spectra. Furthermore, the capability of detecting mental ions was evaluated, founding the CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+ and the change of fluorescence intensity could quantify the concentration of Fe3+. Finally, the biocompatibility and bioimaging test were carried on HepG2 cells, found they were nontoxic and could label the cells with bright blue fluorescence.

Elm seeds were collected from elm trees in Dalian, China. FeCl3, SnCl2·4H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Pb(NO3)2, NaCl, FeCl2·4H2O, CaCl2, AgNO3, MnSO4·H2O, MgSO4·7H2O, K2SO4, ZnCl2 and BaCl2 were purchased from Adamas-Beta Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Black opaque 96-well plates were bought from Jet Bio-Filtration Co., ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Trypsin, Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), penicillin and streptomycin were bought from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., ltd. (Beijing, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Zhejiang Tianhang Sijiqing Biotechnology Co., ltd., (Hangzhou, China). 96-well plates (In vitro scientific) were obtained from Xinyou Biotechnology Co., ltd. (Hangzhou, China). 6-well plates were obtained from Nest Biotechnology Co., ltd. (Wuxi, China). All of the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

CDs were synthesized according to previous reported hydrothermal method [15,24]. Fresh elm seeds were collected from the elm trees and washed three times by deionized water. Then, 50 g elm seeds were mashed into a paste using a mortar, followed by adding 200 mL deionized water and stirring for 20 min on a magnetic stirrer. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 20 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the dark brown solution was poured into a beaker and filtered by 0.22 μm syringe filter. After that, the solution was further purified by Sephadex G-25 gel filtration chromatography with pure water as the eluent. The fractions with bright blue fluorescence under UV light were collected and lyophilized to obtain the desired CDs.

The morphology of CDs was imaged by a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscopic, and the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The particle size distribution was calculated from the TEM images by ImageJ software. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) spectra were carried out on an IRPrestige-2 FTIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurement was recorded on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The fluorescence spectra and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum were measured by a Synergy H1 Hybrid Multi-Mode Microplate Reader (BioTek, Winooski, VT, USA).

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured by a relative method, using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the reference (QY = 0.54). Briefly, certain amounts of CDs and quinine sulfate were respectively dissolved in water and 0.1 M H2SO4. The absorbance values at 325 nm were measured, which should be kept under 0.05. Furthermore, the integral fluorescence intensities of CDs and quinine sulfate were recorded within the emission wavelength of 370–600 nm, excited at the wavelength of 325 nm. The QY was calculated by according to the following equation:
(1)
Q Y =
Q Y
Ref
×
I
I
Ref
×
A
Ref
A
×
η
2
η
Ref
2
Where QY is the quantum yield, I is the measured integral fluorescence intensity, A refers to the absorbance. In addition, η presents the refractive index of the solvent, which were 1.33 for both CDs solution and quinine sulfate solution. The subscript Ref refers to the corresponding parameter of quinine sulfate.

To evaluate the pH stability, desired amounts CDs were dissolved in solutions in the range of 1–14 (containing HCl or NaOH). The fluorescence intensities were then measured by a microplate reader, with the excitation wavelength at 324 nm and the emission wavelength at 392 nm.

Similarly, the CDs solution was added in a black opaque 96-well plate, and continuously irradiated by a UV lamp for 4 h at the wavelength of 365 nm. The fluorescence intensities were then measured by a microplate reader, with the excitation wavelength at 324 nm and the emission wavelength at 392 nm.

To demonstrate the selectivity of CDs on metal ions, various metal ions were mixed with CDs to compare their effect on the fluorescence intensities of CDs. Briefly, CDs solution (100 μL) was firstly added in a black opaque 96-well plate. Then, 100 μL metal ions solutions (Ba2+, Zn2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Na+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Sn2+ or Fe3+, 2 mM) were respectively added. The plate was incubated for 20 min, 37 °C on a shaker, and subsequently measured the fluorescence intensities by a BioTek microplate reader. The excitation wavelength λex was set at 324 nm, while the emission wavelength λem was set at 392 nm. Furthermore, different amounts of Fe3+ (0–1 mM) were also incubated with CDs in the same manner, and the fluorescence intensities were recorded.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of CDs, HepG2 cells were performed to test the cytotoxicity of CDs by MTT assay. Generally, HepG2 cells were cultured in DMEM (containing 10 % FBS, 1 % penicillin and 1 % streptomycin) and incubated in CO2 (5 %) at 37 °C. HepG2 cells in the logarithmic phase of growth were firstly seeded in a 96-well plate with the density of 1 × 104 cells/well. The cells were cultured for 24 h and the medium was replaced with fresh DMEM. Then, CDs were added into each well, forming the final concentration of 15.6, 31.2, 62.5, 125, 250, 500 and 1000 µg/mL. After incubated for another 24 h, the medium was discarded and MTT (5 mg/mL) contained DEME was added in each well. The cells were incubated for additional 4 h and then replace the supernatant with 200 μL DMSO to dissolve the formazan crystals. At last, the absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader.

HepG2 cells in the logarithmic phase of growth were seeded in a 6-well glass bottom plate (2 × 105 cells/well) and cultured in 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h. The supernatant was later removed and washed with PBS for three times. After that, fresh DMEM containing 1 mg/ml CDs was added and incubated for another 12 h. At last, the cells were washed with PBS for three times and observed by an inverted fluorescence microscope (IX81, Olympus, Tokyo, Japan).

The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using elm seed as carbon sources. The cooled dark brown solution was firstly filtered by 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove large particles, and then purified by Sephadex G-25 gel filtration chromatography. The obtained CDs solution was light brown under daylight and exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. In order to identify the structural characterizations of CDs, TEM, FTIR and XPS were respectively conducted. The morphology of CDs was firstly characterized by TEM (see Fig. 1 ), founding they were near-spherical in shape and well dispersed. In addition, the size distribution of CDs was calculated and found the majority of CDs fell within the range of 2–6 nm with the average diameter of 3.83 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1 B) clearly exhibited the lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which was attributed to the (100) facet of graphite [25]. The results of TEM and HRTEM demonstrated the CDs were small, uniform and homogeneously dispersed in water, with graphite-like structure. Furthermore, the surface functional groups of CDs were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy. As seen in Fig. 2 , the broad band at 3370 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, where a small peak at 2965 cm−1 was corresponding to the CH stretching of aliphatic compounds. The amide Ⅱ band at 1670 cm−1 was ascribed to the bending vibration of CO and the stretching vibration of CN. Furthermore, the peaks at 1597 cm−1, 1452 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 were originated from the stretching vibrations of CC, CN and CN, respectively. Additional peak at 1095 cm−1 was also observed, indicating the present of COC and COH bonds. Apart from FTIR, the chemical composition and structure of CDs were also determined by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 3
A, three strong peaks at 283.08, 400.08 and 532.08 eV was observed, which respectively corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s. The percentages of C, N and O elements in CDs were calculated to be 68.35 %, 9.72 % and 21.93 %, respectively. Furthermore, the high solution XPS of each element was further performed and fitted to confirm the structure. The high solution C1s spectrum (Fig. 4
B) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.68, 285.98, and 287.83 eV, which were respectively ascribed to the groups of sp2 C (CC or CC), CO and OCO/OCNH [26,27]. The high solution N1s spectrum (Fig. 4 C) were deconvoluted into the peaks at 399.7, and 401.65 eV, corresponding to the bonds of pyrrolic N and the graphitic N. Also, the deconvolution analysis of high solution O1s spectrum (Fig. 4 D) confirmed the existence of CO band (531.05 eV) and CO band (531.96 eV). The results of FTIR and XPS both demonstrated that the CDs contained abundant nitrogen and oxygen elements, which formed the functional carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and amide groups. The existence of various hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the CDs could endow them with good water solubility and enhanced fluorescence properties [28].

The optical properties of CDs were further detected to evaluate their potential applications. The solution of CDs was light brown with the concentration of 1 mg/mL, which exhibited bright blue fluorescence under UV lamp (Fig. 3 A). The UV–vis absorption spectrum in Fig. 3 B showed three weak peaks, where the peak at 248 nm was ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds and the peaks at 270 and in 327 nm were originated from the n-π* transition of CO bonds [29]. Furthermore, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were measured, founding the maximum excitation wavelength (λex) at 324 nm and the maximum emission wavelength (λem) at 392 nm. In order to better understand the fluorescent properties, the emission spectra of CDs under various excitation wavelengths were recorded. As seen in Fig. 3 C, the emission spectrum of CDs was adjustable and highly excitation-dependent. As the excitation wavelength gradually increased from 320 nm to 500 nm (20 nm of increment), the emission peaks were correspondingly red-shifted from 400 nm to 550 nm, with the intensities gradually decreased. It was obvious that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was strong in the region of blue color other than yellow and green. Furthermore, the quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated to be 6.15 % using quinine sulfate as the standard.

The pH stability and photostability of CDs were further evaluated, by comparing the relative fluorescence intensities under different pH conditions or UV irradiation. As exhibited in Fig. 5
A, the change of fluorescence intensities were less than 20 % in the range of pH 1–14, indicating the good pH stability. Similarly, the photostability of CDs was found excellent, as they could maintain up to 80 % of the fluorescence intensity after continuously irradiated for 4 h by UV lamp (Fig. 5 B).

CDs have been widely applied as the sensors of metal ions, because the functional hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CDs could form complexes with metal ions [30]. The occupation of functional groups by metal ions could be quantitatively responded by the decrease or enhance of fluorescence intensity. Hence, various metal ions could be selectively detected by CDs, including Fe3+, Ag+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Cr6+. In this work, the potential application of CDs as metal ions sensor was firstly evaluated. CDs were respectively mixed with numerous metal ions (Ba2+, Zn2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Na+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Sn2+ or Fe3+) and compared their fluorescence intensity (F) with that of blank CDs (F0). As shown in Fig. 6
A, the F/F0 value was selected as the parameter to reflect the effect of metal ions on CDs, founding the value of F/F0 was near 1 for most ions. However, the F/F0 was deceased to less than 0.2 in case the CDs were mixed with Fe3+. This demonstrated that the CDs could be significantly and selectively quenched by Fe3+. Fig. 6 B exhibited the digital photographs of blank CDs solution and that mixed with 1 mM Fe3+, which were irradiated by a UV lamp. It was obvious that the blue fluorescence was disappeared in the present of Fe3+. The mechanism of quenching might be caused by the electron transfer between CDs and Fe3+, where the hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface acted as the electron donors and Fe3+ as the electron acceptor. The coordination interaction between CDs and Fe3+ could break the radiative jump and lead to the excited electrons transferred from CDs to the 3d orbit of Fe3+, finally resulting in the fluorescence quenching [28,31,32]. In order to evaluate the feasibility CDs as Fe3+ sensor, we further studied the relationship between Fe3+ concentration and fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 6 C, the fluorescent intensity of CDs (λex = 324 nm, λem = 392 nm.) was gradually from 25,000 to 5000 (decreased by 80 %), when the concentration of Fe3+ gradually increased from 0 to 0.5 mM. Moreover, the Fig. 6 D showed a good linearity between the value of F0/F and Fe3+ concentration, with the following calibration equation:
(2) F0/F = 9.04C(F3+) + 1.03, R2 = 0.9907
Where F or F0 was the fluorescent intensities of CDs with or without Fe3+, and C(F3+) was the concentration of Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.18 μM in the range of 0–500 μM, deriving from the 3σ/S of blank solution. Where S was the slope of the calibration plot and σ was the standard deviation of the signals (n = 11). The LOD was lower than the iron concentrations of the standard in drinking water (5.4 μM, regulated by the WHO) and blood (20–29 μM) [33]. Table 1 compared the Fe3+ detection capability of the obtained CDs with various reported biomass-based CDs, in which the linear range was ordered from small to large range. It is obvious that the obtained CDs from elm seeds have a quite broad linear range and a low LOD to detect Fe3+. The data fully demonstrated that the CDs could selectively detect the concentration of Fe3+ with high sensitivity, and potentially applied for water pollution detection such as urban sewage and industrial waste.

As CDs have good biocompatibilities, they have been developed as many charming tools for biological applications including bioimaging, drug delivery or disease treatment. In this work, the biocompatibility of CDs was evaluated by MTT assay on HepG2 cells. CDs with different concentrations were firstly incubated with HepG2 cells in 96-well plates for 24 h, and the cellular viability was calculated by measuring the amount of formazan. Fig. 7 exhibited the cellular viabilities of HepG2 cells treated by CDs, with the concentration of 0–1000 μg/mL. Obviously, the viabilities of CDs treated HepG2 cells in all groups were almost 100 %, even the amount of CDs had reached to 1000 μg/mL. This indicated the toxicity of CDs was negligible, which ensured them being potentially use for bioimaging and other biological applications.

After that, the capability of CDs for bioimaging was further evaluated. HepG2 cells were incubated with CDs (1 mg/ml) for 12 h and observed by an inverted fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 8 , the morphology of cell was not significantly changed or damaged by CDs, which confirmed the low toxicity of CDs. The cell were respectively excited and observed in blue, green and red channels, founding the cells just emitted bright blue fluorescence with negligible green and red fluorescence. The weak green and red fluorescence signals could be explained by the fluorescence spectra of CDs, as the intensities were very low once the excitation wavelength over 480 nm (Fig. 4 C). In the merged image, it was obvious that the blue fluorescence was mainly distributed in the cytoplasm rather than the nucleus of cells. This revealed that the CDs could effectively pass through the cell membrane and their fluorescence properties could be stably remained. Overall, the CDs synthesized from elm seeds could exhibit bright blue fluorescence in human cells with excellent biocompatibility, which might be potentially applied as bioimaging agents.

In this work, we successfully synthesized CDs by hydrothermal method, using natural siberian elm (Ulmus pumila L.) seeds as carbon source for the first time. The obtained CDs were near-spherical in shape with an average diameter of 3.83 nm. They were mainly composed by carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, which formed the various hydrophilic functional carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and amide groups on the surface. In addition, the CDs could emit bright blue fluorescence under UV lamp with the maximum emission wavelength at 392 nm. Furthermore, they exhibited excellent pH stability and photostability. Among various metal ions, the CDs could be selectively quenched by Fe3+, and the concentration of Fe3+ could be sensitivity reflected by the value of F0/F with the LOD of 3.18 μM in the range of 0–500 μM. At last, the CDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility and could emit bright blue fluorescence in HepG2 cells. In summary, the CDs could be potentially applied as Fe3+ sensors for water pollution detection and applied agents for cell imaging.

Jianbin Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Guoshuang Zheng: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yan Tian: Visualization, Investigation. Chenghong Zhang: Software. Yuting Wang: Validation. Mingjie Liu: Formal analysis. Dongze Ren: Methodology, Formal analysis. Huijun Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Weiting Yu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 81903560), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant number 2021MD703801) and the Dalian Young Star of Science and Technology Project (grant number 2018RQ81).

Anal. Methods

Inorg. Chem. Commun.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable Hydrothermal and Solvothermal Synthesis of Advanced Carbon Materials in Multidimensional Applications: A Review
